name: An algebraic interpretation of effective order
subsection_title: An algebraic interpretation of effective order
statement_text:
A Runge–Kutta method with corresponding group element α
has effective order p if and only if (389a) holds, where β is such that β(τ ) = 0.
proof_text:

context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
The group $G_1/(1+H_p)$ has cosets representing methods that agree to $O(h^{p+1})$ in one step, with $E(1+H_p)$ being the coset of methods that match the exact solution to that order. A method with group element $\alpha$ has effective order $p$ if there exists a perturbing method $\beta$ such that the conjugacy condition
\[
\beta\alpha\beta^{-1} \in E(1 + H_p)
\]
holds. Here $G_1$ is the group of Runge--Kutta methods, $H_p$ is a subgroup of $G_1$, and $p$ denotes the (effective) order.

variables.name: α
variables.definition: group element corresponding to a Runge–Kutta method

variables.name: β
variables.definition: group element corresponding to a perturbing Runge–Kutta method

variables.name: E
variables.definition: group element corresponding to the exact solution method

variables.name: Hp
variables.definition: subgroup of G1 such that G1/(1+Hp) consists of cosets of methods agreeing to O(h^{p+1}) in a single step

variables.name: p
variables.definition: order or effective order of a method

preamble: The text uses group theory to analyze effective order of Runge–Kutta methods. The group G1/(1+Hp) has cosets representing methods that agree to O(h^{p+1}) in one step, with E(1+Hp) being the coset of methods that match the exact solution to that order. A method with group element α has effective order p if there exists a perturbing method β such that the conjugacy condition βαβ^{-1} ∈ E(1+Hp) holds.

D is normal subgroup of G1
